facts.text: The 1960s were a turning-point decade when old studio systems weakened and more daring filmmaking took over.
Alfred Hitchcock's Psycho premiered in 1960 and changed horror filmmaking forever with its shocking structure and shower scene.
West Side Story won the Academy Award for Best Picture for films released in 1961.
Lawrence of Arabia (1962) is celebrated for its epic desert cinematography and sweeping score by Maurice Jarre.
Dr. No (1962) introduced Sean Connery as James Bond and launched one of cinema's longest-running franchises.
To Kill a Mockingbird (1962) earned Gregory Peck an Oscar for his performance as Atticus Finch.
The Great Escape (1963) helped cement Steve McQueen's image as a major movie star.
Mary Poppins (1964) blended live action and animation in a way that amazed audiences of the era.
Goldfinger (1964) established many of the classic James Bond formula elements, from gadgets to the theme song.
The Sound of Music won Best Picture for films released in 1965 and became one of the biggest box-office hits of the decade.
The Good, the Bad and the Ugly (1966) helped define the spaghetti western through Sergio Leone's style and Ennio Morricone's music.
Bonnie and Clyde (1967) pushed violence and antihero storytelling into mainstream American cinema.
The Graduate (1967) became a cultural landmark with Dustin Hoffman, Anne Bancroft, and a Simon & Garfunkel soundtrack.
Night of the Living Dead (1968) essentially created the modern zombie movie as audiences know it today.
2001: A Space Odyssey (1968) stunned viewers with groundbreaking visual effects made without CGI.
Planet of the Apes (1968) wowed audiences with advanced ape makeup and one of the most famous twist endings in movie history.
Midnight Cowboy, released in 1969, became the only X-rated film ever to win Best Picture.
Easy Rider (1969) became a defining New Hollywood film by proving counterculture stories could be major hits.
Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid (1969) paired Paul Newman and Robert Redford in one of the decade's most beloved buddy films.
The MPAA ratings system replaced the old Production Code in 1968, reshaping how American films were marketed and made.
The late 1960s opened the door to a more director-driven era that would explode in the 1970s.
Alfred Hitchcock's Psycho (1960) changed horror cinema with its editing, suspense, and shocking structure.
Its shower scene became one of the most discussed sequences in film history.
Psycho is one of the signature titles or subjects people mention when discussing 60s Movies.
Any overview of 60s Movies usually makes room for Psycho.
Psycho helped define the tone and reputation of 60s Movies.
Fans exploring 60s Movies are often encouraged to start with Psycho.
The legacy of 60s Movies is hard to explain without mentioning Psycho.
Critical conversations about 60s Movies often return to Psycho.
For many fans, Psycho captures part of why 60s Movies remains so memorable.
Lists, retrospectives, and fan rankings about 60s Movies frequently include Psycho.
